name: Filmore Hill
overview: Filmore Hill is a rural hamlet of around 28 residents in the civil parish of Froxfield and Privett, in the East Hampshire district, sitting at the chalk-down top of Stoner Hill within the South Downs National Park. The postcode GU34 3NX is correctly tagged in the seed data, though geographically the hamlet is much closer to Petersfield (6.8 mi south-east) than to Alton (around ten miles north) — buyers should treat Petersfield as the practical hub town despite the seed list grouping. The parish climbs to over 240 m on its northern boundary and is separated from Petersfield by the Stoner Hill scarp; the Basing Park estate lies in the north-west of the parish. The most prominent building is the 18th-century Grade II-listed Filmore Hill Farmhouse, a brick former farmhouse still in private ownership. The nearest village amenities — church, hall and shop — sit at Privett and the Meon Hut crossroads, about a mile and a quarter away.
character: Dispersed cluster of large detached farmhouses and barn conversions along a single chalk lane, with frontages of brick, flint and the occasional cob-and-thatch survivor. The locality type is officially 'rural hamlet and isolated dwellings'. Plot sizes are large, planning constraints are exceptionally tight given SDNP designation, and turnover is rare; recent detached sales in GU34 3NX have averaged in the high £800,000s.
amenities: Privett shop and village hall (1.2 mi)
Gigabit-capable broadband at one or more premises

schools.name: West Meon Church of England Voluntary Controlled Primary School

transport.roads: A272 ~1 mi; A3 (Petersfield) ~7 mi; A32 ~4 mi

thingsToDo: South Downs Way walking from Stoner Hill
West Meon vineyards and Hambledon Vineyard tour (~6 mi)
Petersfield Heath and Petersfield Pond (~7 mi)

placesToEat.name: The Pub With No Name (White Horse Inn), Priors Dean
placesToEat.notes: Famously named for its missing sign, ~3 mi north

pros: Inside the South Downs National Park with strong planning protection
Gigabit broadband at one or more premises (rare for a chalk hamlet)
Petersfield mainline 6.8 mi for direct Waterloo trains
Substantial period properties with generous land
cons: Seed data lists Alton as hub town; Petersfield is the real centre
No shop, school, pub or village hall within the hamlet
Property stock is small and rarely turns over
Steep lanes around Stoner Hill can ice in winter
whoItSuits: Cash-rich buyers wanting a National Park position with substantial period stock and accepting that everyday life requires a car. The combination of SDNP designation, listed houses and seven property sales since 1995 means stock is small, slow-moving and priced accordingly. Best fit is downsizing-up households moving from London or south-east commuter towns, retirees, or remote workers who genuinely value the gigabit-capable broadband. Poor fit for first-time buyers or anyone reliant on public transport.
